title: Exception flow and stack traces

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Exception flow and stack traces; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Exceptions and Java I/O learning paths; atomic topic: Exception flow and stack traces
sources.claim: Java exceptions propagate up call frames until a compatible handler is reached; responsible boundaries catch only failures they can translate, recover from, or enrich without losing the cause.

sources.locator: Java Language Specification, Java SE 21; Java SE 21; JLS chapter 11: exception kinds, checking, causes, and abrupt completion; atomic topic: Exception flow and stack traces
sources.claim: Java exceptions propagate up call frames until a compatible handler is reached; responsible boundaries catch only failures they can translate, recover from, or enrich without losing the cause.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Java exceptions propagate up call frames until a compatible handler is reached; responsible boundaries catch only failures they can translate, recover from, or enrich without losing the cause.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A repository method lets an IOException propagate, while a service wraps it in a domain exception with the original cause and useful operation context. Your goal is to trace exception propagation and preserve useful cause and context. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and invariants; String and text processing; Method contracts and parameters; Compiler and runtime diagnostics. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Java exceptions propagate up call frames until a compatible handler is reached; responsible boundaries catch only failures they can translate, recover from, or enrich without losing the cause. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: finally and try-with-resources affect cleanup, a catch order must place specific types first, suppressed exceptions can accompany resource failure, and broad catches can hide programming defects. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Catching Exception at every method makes a program robust because no failure can escape.

includes: Throw, propagate and catch exceptions at responsible abstraction boundaries.
Trace exception propagation and preserve useful cause and context.
excludes: finally and try-with-resources affect cleanup, a catch order must place specific types first, suppressed exceptions can accompany resource failure, and broad catches can hide programming defects.

outcomes: Trace exception propagation and preserve useful cause and context.

examples.title: Exception flow and stack traces: direct language case
examples.situation: A repository method lets an IOException propagate, while a service wraps it in a domain exception with the original cause and useful operation context.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A repository method lets an IOException propagate, while a service wraps it in a domain exception with the original cause and useful operation context.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Catch an exception only at a responsible boundary, preserve its cause and context, and let unhandled failures propagate to a policy that can act.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Catch an exception only at a responsible boundary, preserve its cause and context, and let unhandled failures propagate to a policy that can act.

examples.title: Exception flow and stack traces: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A parser catches NumberFormatException at the input boundary, reports the invalid field, and does not catch unrelated RuntimeException values as ordinary input failure.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A parser catches NumberFormatException at the input boundary, reports the invalid field, and does not catch unrelated RuntimeException values as ordinary input failure.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: finally and try-with-resources affect cleanup, a catch order must place specific types first, suppressed exceptions can accompany resource failure, and broad catches can hide programming defects.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Catching Exception at every method makes a program robust because no failure can escape.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Trace exception propagation and preserve useful cause and context.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Catching Exception at every method makes a program robust because no failure can escape.
misconceptions.correction: Catch an exception only at a responsible boundary, preserve its cause and context, and let unhandled failures propagate to a policy that can act.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Catching Exception at every method makes a program robust because no failure can escape.

analogy.example: a document desk that records a failure chain, handles only recoverable cases, and always closes borrowed equipment can introduce Exception flow and stack traces, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: exception boundaries, paths, text encodings, and resource cleanup
limitations: suppressed exceptions, stack unwinding, filesystem semantics, and serialization attacks need exact technical treatment
finally and try-with-resources affect cleanup, a catch order must place specific types first, suppressed exceptions can accompany resource failure, and broad catches can hide programming defects.

recap.summary: You should now be able to trace exception propagation and preserve useful cause and context. Keep this boundary in view: finally and try-with-resources affect cleanup, a catch order must place specific types first, suppressed exceptions can accompany resource failure, and broad catches can hide programming defects.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Exception flow and stack traces using canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate terms and one observable consequence.
Why is this belief incorrect: Catching Exception at every method makes a program robust because no failure can escape.
